expanded_description: The library of common functions, often called parent functions or toolkit functions, forms the visual vocabulary of mathematics. Just as knowing the alphabet is essential for reading, recognizing the basic shapes of functions like lines, parabolas, cubics, square roots, and exponentials is fundamental for interpreting graphs, understanding transformations, and analyzing behavior in Pre-calculus and Calculus. These functions serve as the building blocks for more complex mathematical models; nearly every function encountered in advanced mathematics can be understood as a transformation (shifting, stretching, reflecting) of one of these parent functions. Mastery of their graphs involves not just memorizing shapes, but understanding key characteristics: domain and range, intercepts, symmetry (even, odd, or neither), intervals of increase and decrease, end behavior, and asymptotes. This visual literacy enables students to quickly sketch graphs without plotting numerous points, predict the behavior of transformed functions, and interpret real-world data modeled by these functions. In physics, quadratics model projectile motion; in biology, exponentials model population growth; in economics, linear functions model cost relationships. A deep familiarity with this function library is indispensable for success in graphing transformations, solving equations graphically, and grasping the geometric intuition behind limits and derivatives.

core_concept.principle:
Parent Functions: A set of basic functions with distinctive graphs that serve as templates for families of functions.

Key Functions in the Library:
- Constant: $f(x) = c$ (Horizontal line)
- Identity/Linear: $f(x) = x$ (Diagonal line through origin)
- Quadratic: $f(x) = x^2$ (Parabola opening up)
- Cubic: $f(x) = x^3$ (S-shaped curve through origin)
- Square Root: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ (Half-parabola on side)
- Cube Root: $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ (S-shaped curve, steeper than cubic)
- Absolute Value: $f(x) = |x|$ (V-shape)
- Reciprocal: $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ (Hyperbola with asymptotes)
- Exponential: $f(x) = b^x$ ($b > 1$) (Rapid growth)
- Logarithmic: $f(x) = \log_b(x)$ (Inverse of exponential)

Key Characteristics to Identify:
- Shape: Visual appearance (line, curve, V-shape, etc.)
- Domain & Range: Allowed inputs and possible outputs
- Intercepts: Where graph crosses axes
- Symmetry: Even (y-axis), Odd (origin), or Neither
- End Behavior: What happens as $x \to \pm\infty$
- Asymptotes: Lines the graph approaches but does not touch
core_concept.explanation: Think of parent functions as the 'primitive shapes' of algebra. Just as a carpenter knows what a board, a nail, and a screw look like before building a cabinet, a mathematician must know what a parabola, exponential, or reciprocal graph looks like before analyzing complex equations. When you see $y = (x-2)^2 + 3$, you shouldn't start plotting points from scratch; you should recognize it as a Quadratic Parent Function that has been moved. This recognition saves time and reduces errors. Each parent function has a 'personality': the Quadratic is symmetric and has a turning point; the Exponential grows explosively in one direction and hugs an asymptote in the other; the Reciprocal breaks at zero and lives in two separate pieces. Understanding these personalities allows you to predict behavior. For instance, knowing the Square Root function starts at $(0,0)$ and increases slowly tells you that $\sqrt{x-5}$ must start at $(5,0)$. This visual intuition is the bridge between algebraic formulas and geometric understanding, critical for calculus where graph behavior determines limits and integrals.

image.alt: Overview of the library of parent functions
image.caption: Figure 2.4.1: The family of parent functions showing basic shapes and equations.

sections.section_title: 2.4.1 Polynomial Parent Functions: Linear, Quadratic, Cubic
sections.introduction: Polynomial functions are among the most fundamental in mathematics, characterized by non-negative integer powers of $x$. The three most basic polynomial parent functions are the Linear (Identity), Quadratic, and Cubic functions. These functions are continuous and smooth everywhere, with domains of all real numbers. Their graphs exhibit distinct shapes and symmetry properties that make them easily recognizable. Mastery of these three graphs provides the foundation for understanding higher-degree polynomials and their transformations.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.4.2: Graphs of Linear, Quadratic, and Cubic parent functions.

key_concept.definition:
Linear (Identity) Function: $f(x) = x$
- Graph: Straight line passing through origin with slope 1.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Odd (symmetric about origin)
- Behavior: Increasing everywhere.

Quadratic Function: $f(x) = x^2$
- Graph: Parabola opening upward with vertex at origin.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $[0, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Even (symmetric about y-axis)
- Behavior: Decreasing on $(-\infty, 0)$, Increasing on $(0, \infty)$.

Cubic Function: $f(x) = x^3$
- Graph: S-shaped curve passing through origin, flattening near origin.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Odd (symmetric about origin)
- Behavior: Increasing everywhere, but rate changes.
key_concept.context: These three functions represent degrees 1, 2, and 3. The degree determines the end behavior: odd degrees (Linear, Cubic) go in opposite directions at ends ($\infty$ and $-\infty$); even degrees (Quadratic) go in the same direction (both $\infty$ or both $-\infty$). In physics, linear functions model constant velocity, quadratics model constant acceleration (gravity), and cubics model more complex motion profiles. In economics, quadratics often model profit maximization. Recognizing these shapes instantly allows students to sketch transformations without tables of values.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.3: Comparison of domain, range, symmetry, and behavior for linear, quadratic, and cubic functions.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Polynomial Parent Functions

image.caption: Figure 2.4.4: Matching equations to graph shapes.

worked_examples.problem:
Match each equation to its graph description:
a) $f(x) = x^2$
b) $f(x) = x^3$
c) $f(x) = x$

step_by_step.explanation: Even power means outputs are non-negative. Symmetric about y-axis.
step_by_step.calculation: Graph: Parabola opening upward, vertex at (0,0).

step_by_step.explanation: Odd power preserves sign. Negative inputs give negative outputs.
step_by_step.calculation: Graph: S-shaped curve through origin, Quadrants I and III.

step_by_step.explanation: Linear relationship. Constant rate of change.
step_by_step.calculation: Graph: Straight diagonal line through origin, slope 1.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Parabola; b) S-curve; c) Straight line
worked_examples.concept_applied: The degree and parity (even/odd) of the power determine the basic shape and symmetry of polynomial parent functions.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing End Behavior

image.caption: Figure 2.4.5: Describing behavior as x approaches infinity.

worked_examples.problem:
Describe the end behavior of each parent function:
a) $f(x) = x^2$
b) $f(x) = x^3$

step_by_step.explanation: As x gets very large (positive or negative), $x^2$ becomes very large positive.
As x gets large positive, $x^3$ is large positive. As x gets large negative, $x^3$ is large negative.
Even degree: same direction. Odd degree: opposite directions.

worked_examples.concept_applied: End behavior is determined by the degree (even/odd) and leading coefficient sign of the polynomial.

worked_examples.title: Determining Symmetry

image.caption: Figure 2.4.6: Testing for even and odd symmetry.

worked_examples.problem:
Determine if each function is Even, Odd, or Neither:
a) $f(x) = x^2$
b) $f(x) = x^3$
c) $f(x) = x + 1$

step_by_step.calculation: Since $f(-x) = f(x)$, it is Even (y-axis symmetry).

step_by_step.explanation: Check $f(-x)$. $-x + 1$. This is neither $f(x)$ nor $-f(x)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Even; b) Odd; c) Neither
worked_examples.concept_applied: Even functions satisfy $f(-x) = f(x)$; Odd functions satisfy $f(-x) = -f(x)$. Parent polynomials $x^n$ are even if n is even, odd if n is odd.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.7: Summary of linear, quadratic, and cubic parent functions.

sections.introduction: Radical functions (involving roots) and the Absolute Value function introduce restrictions and sharp turns not seen in polynomials. The Square Root function is defined only for non-negative inputs, creating a graph that starts at a specific point and extends infinitely in one direction. The Cube Root function, like the cubic, is defined for all reals but has a vertical tangent at the origin. The Absolute Value function creates a V-shape due to its piecewise definition, representing distance from zero. These functions are crucial for modeling constraints and magnitudes.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to radical and absolute value functions
introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.4.8: Graphs of Square Root, Cube Root, and Absolute Value functions.

key_concept.definition:
Square Root Function: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$
- Graph: Starts at $(0, 0)$, curves to the right, increasing slowly.
- Domain: $[0, \infty)$
- Range: $[0, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Neither
- Behavior: Increasing on $(0, \infty)$.

Cube Root Function: $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$
- Graph: S-shaped curve through origin, vertical at origin.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Odd (origin symmetry)
- Behavior: Increasing everywhere.

Absolute Value Function: $f(x) = |x|$
- Graph: V-shape with vertex at $(0, 0)$.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $[0, \infty)$
- Intercepts: $(0, 0)$
- Symmetry: Even (y-axis symmetry)
- Behavior: Decreasing on $(-\infty, 0)$, Increasing on $(0, \infty)$.
key_concept.context: The Square Root function is the inverse of the Quadratic (restricted to $x \geq 0$). The Cube Root is the inverse of the Cubic. The Absolute Value function represents distance, so it is never negative. In physics, absolute value models magnitude (speed vs velocity). Square roots appear in distance formulas and standard deviations. Cube roots appear in volume-to-side-length calculations. Recognizing the 'starting point' of the square root graph or the 'corner' of the absolute value graph is key for graphing transformations later.

image.alt: Properties of radical and absolute value functions
image.caption: Figure 2.4.9: Domain, range, and shape comparison for radical and absolute value parents.

worked_examples.title: Sketching Radical Functions

image.caption: Figure 2.4.10: Plotting key points for square root and cube root.

worked_examples.problem: Sketch $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ and $g(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ by plotting key points.

step_by_step.explanation: Choose perfect squares for x: 0, 1, 4, 9.
step_by_step.calculation: Points: $(0,0), (1,1), (4,2), (9,3)$. Connect with smooth curve starting at 0.

step_by_step.explanation: Choose perfect cubes for x: -8, -1, 0, 1, 8.
Square root exists only for $x \geq 0$. Cube root exists for all x.
step_by_step.calculation: Square root is half-parabola on side; Cube root is full S-curve.

worked_examples.final_answer: Graphs sketched using perfect square/cube points.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Use perfect powers to find integer coordinates for accurate sketching of radical functions.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Absolute Value Graph

image.caption: Figure 2.4.11: Identifying vertex and symmetry of absolute value.

step_by_step.explanation: The lowest point where the direction changes.
Fold along y-axis. Left side matches right side.
Look at slope on left and right.

worked_examples.final_answer: Vertex (0,0), Even symmetry, Decreasing then Increasing
worked_examples.concept_applied: Absolute value graphs have a sharp vertex and are symmetric about the vertical line through the vertex.

worked_examples.title: Domain and Range of Radicals

image.alt: Example domain and range of radicals
image.caption: Figure 2.4.12: Determining domain and range from radical graphs.

worked_examples.problem: State the domain and range for $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ and $g(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Graph starts at x=0 and goes right. y starts at 0 and goes up.
Graph extends infinitely left/right and up/down.
Even roots have restrictions; odd roots do not.
step_by_step.calculation: Index of root determines domain restrictions.

worked_examples.final_answer: sqrt: D:[0,inf), R:[0,inf); cbrt: D:R, R:R
worked_examples.concept_applied: Even index radicals (square root) require non-negative radicands; odd index radicals (cube root) accept all real numbers.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.13: Summary of square root, cube root, and absolute value functions.

sections.introduction: Rational and transcendental functions introduce asymptotes and exponential growth/decay, behaviors not found in polynomials or radicals. The Reciprocal function creates a hyperbola with breaks at the axes. Exponential functions model rapid growth or decay, always staying positive. Logarithmic functions are the inverses of exponentials, growing slowly and existing only for positive inputs. These functions are essential for modeling natural phenomena like population dynamics, radioactive decay, sound intensity, and pH levels.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to rational and transcendental functions
introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.4.14: Graphs of Reciprocal, Exponential, and Logarithmic functions.

key_concept.definition:
Reciprocal Function: $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$
- Graph: Hyperbola with two branches in Quadrants I and III.
- Domain: $(-\infty, 0) \cup (0, \infty)$
- Range: $(-\infty, 0) \cup (0, \infty)$
- Asymptotes: Vertical ($x=0$), Horizontal ($y=0$)
- Symmetry: Odd (origin symmetry)

Exponential Function: $f(x) = b^x$ ($b > 1$)
- Graph: Curve increasing rapidly, hugging x-axis on left.
- Domain: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Range: $(0, \infty)$
- Asymptote: Horizontal ($y=0$)
- Intercept: $(0, 1)$
- Behavior: Increasing everywhere.

Logarithmic Function: $f(x) = \log_b(x)$ ($b > 1$)
- Graph: Curve increasing slowly, vertical at y-axis.
- Domain: $(0, \infty)$
- Range: $(-\infty, \infty)$
- Asymptote: Vertical ($x=0$)
- Intercept: $(1, 0)$
- Behavior: Increasing everywhere.
key_concept.context: The Reciprocal function is the parent of all rational functions; its asymptotes define the boundaries of the graph. Exponential functions are unique because the variable is in the exponent; they model compound interest and population growth. Logarithmic functions 'undo' exponentials; they compress large scales (like the Richter scale). Notice the symmetry between Exponential and Logarithmic: their domains and ranges are swapped, and they are reflections of each other across the line $y=x$. Recognizing asymptotes is critical: the graph gets infinitely close but never touches these lines.

image.alt: Properties of rational and transcendental functions
image.caption: Figure 2.4.15: Asymptotes, domains, and ranges for reciprocal, exponential, and logarithmic parents.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Asymptotes

image.caption: Figure 2.4.16: Locating vertical and horizontal asymptotes.

worked_examples.problem: Identify the vertical and horizontal asymptotes for $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Where is the function undefined? Denominator = 0.
What happens as $x \to \infty$? The value approaches 0.
Graph approaches these lines but never crosses them.
step_by_step.calculation: Asymptotes divide the plane into regions containing the branches.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Vertical asymptotes occur at undefined points; horizontal asymptotes describe end behavior.

worked_examples.title: Exponential vs Logarithmic Features

image.alt: Example comparing exp and log features
image.caption: Figure 2.4.17: Contrasting exponential and logarithmic graphs.

worked_examples.problem: Compare the domain, range, and intercepts of $f(x) = 2^x$ and $g(x) = \log_2(x)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Exponential. Defined for all x. Output always positive.
Logarithmic. Input must be positive. Output can be anything.
Domains and ranges are swapped. Intercepts are swapped.
step_by_step.calculation: They are inverse functions, reflected across $y=x$.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Exponential and Logarithmic functions are inverses; their key features (domain/range, intercepts) are reflections of each other.

worked_examples.title: Sketching Transcendental Graphs

image.caption: Figure 2.4.18: Drawing basic exponential and log curves.

step_by_step.explanation: Horizontal asymptote at $y=0$.
Use integer exponents: $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, 2^{-1}$.
Connect points smoothly, approaching asymptote on left.
step_by_step.calculation: Curve rises rapidly on right, flattens on left.

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph sketched through key points with HA at y=0.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exponential graphs pass through $(0,1)$ and have a horizontal asymptote; use integer powers to plot points.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.19: Summary of reciprocal, exponential, and logarithmic functions.

sections.section_title: 2.4.4 Key Features and Analysis of Function Graphs
sections.introduction: Beyond recognizing shapes, students must be able to analyze and describe the behavior of function graphs using precise mathematical terminology. This includes identifying intervals where the function is increasing or decreasing, locating local and global extrema (maximums and minimums), describing end behavior, and classifying symmetry. These analytical skills are necessary for interpreting graphs in applied contexts and for success in calculus, where these features are quantified using derivatives.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.4.20: Annotating a graph with key features.

key_concept.definition:
Intervals of Increase/Decrease:
- Increasing: Function goes up from left to right ($f(x_2) > f(x_1)$ when $x_2 > x_1$).
- Decreasing: Function goes down from left to right.
- Constant: Function stays flat (horizontal line).

Extrema:
- Local Maximum: Peak point higher than nearby points.
- Local Minimum: Valley point lower than nearby points.
- Global/Absolute: Highest or lowest point on the entire domain.

End Behavior: Describes $f(x)$ as $x \to \infty$ and $x \to -\infty$.
- Notation: As $x \to \infty, f(x) \to \dots$

Continuity:
- Continuous: No breaks, holes, or jumps (can draw without lifting pencil).
- Discontinuous: Has breaks (e.g., Reciprocal function).
key_concept.context: Analyzing features turns a picture into data. In economics, increasing intervals mean growth; decreasing mean decline. Extrema represent optimal values (max profit, min cost). End behavior predicts long-term trends. Continuity ensures predictability; discontinuous functions model sudden changes (tax brackets, shipping costs). In calculus, increasing/decreasing is determined by the sign of the derivative; extrema occur where the derivative is zero. Mastering this vocabulary now makes calculus definitions intuitive later.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.21: Labeling increasing/decreasing intervals, extrema, and end behavior on a graph.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Intervals of Increase and Decrease

image.caption: Figure 2.4.22: Determining where a function rises or falls.

worked_examples.problem: Identify the intervals where $f(x) = x^2$ is increasing and decreasing.

step_by_step.explanation: Parabola opening up with vertex at (0,0).
step_by_step.calculation: Left side goes down, right side goes up.

step_by_step.explanation: As x moves from left to 0, y goes down.
As x moves from 0 to right, y goes up.
At x=0, function is neither increasing nor decreasing.
step_by_step.calculation: Use parentheses for intervals, not brackets.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Read the graph from left to right. Downhill = decreasing, Uphill = increasing. Use open intervals for monotonicity.

worked_examples.title: Describing End Behavior

image.caption: Figure 2.4.23: Using arrow notation for end behavior.

step_by_step.explanation: As $x \to \infty$ (large positive), $-x^3$ becomes large negative.
As $x \to -\infty$ (large negative), $-x^3$ becomes large positive (negative cubed is negative, times negative is positive).
Graph starts high on left, ends low on right.
step_by_step.calculation: Up on left, Down on right.

worked_examples.concept_applied: End behavior describes the direction of the graph's arms at the far left and far right edges of the coordinate plane.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Continuity

image.caption: Figure 2.4.24: Identifying continuous vs discontinuous functions.

worked_examples.problem: Determine if $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ and $g(x) = x^2$ are continuous.

step_by_step.explanation: Graph has a break at x=0 (vertical asymptote).
Graph is a smooth curve with no breaks.
Polynomials are always continuous. Rational functions are discontinuous at zeros of denominator.
step_by_step.calculation: Check for division by zero or piecewise jumps.

worked_examples.concept_applied: A function is continuous if its graph has no holes, jumps, or asymptotes within its domain.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.25: Summary of key features: intervals, extrema, end behavior, continuity.

sections.section_title: 2.4.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: When working with the library of functions, students often confuse similar shapes, misidentify symmetry, or incorrectly describe intervals. Errors in recognizing parent functions lead to mistakes in transformations and calculus. Common pitfalls include mixing up quadratic and absolute value shapes, forgetting asymptote restrictions, misstating intervals of increase/decrease, and confusing domain with range. Recognizing these errors helps develop precision in graph interpretation.

classifications.value: Mixing up graphs of similar-looking functions.
classifications.behavior: Leads to incorrect predictions of behavior and transformations.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.26: Error - confusing quadratic and absolute value graphs.

examples.explanation: Correct: $y=x^2$ is a smooth U-shaped parabola. $y=|x|$ is the V-shape with a sharp corner.
Correct: Exponentials have a horizontal asymptote at $y=0$; they never touch the x-axis.

classifications.value: Using brackets instead of parentheses for monotonicity intervals.
characteristics: Including the turning point in the interval
Using union symbol incorrectly for intervals
classifications.behavior: Mathematically imprecise; turning points are neither increasing nor decreasing.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.27: Error - incorrect bracket usage for intervals.

examples.explanation: Correct: Use open intervals for increase/decrease: $(0, \infty)$. At 0, slope is zero.

classifications.value: Incorrectly classifying functions as Even or Odd.
characteristics: Assuming all polynomials are Even or Odd
Confusing origin symmetry with y-axis symmetry
classifications.behavior: Leads to incorrect graphing and integration properties.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.28: Error - misidentifying symmetry types.

examples.process: Error: Claiming $f(x) = x^2 + x$ is Even.
examples.explanation: Correct: Mixed powers make it Neither. Test $f(-x) = x^2 - x \neq f(x)$.

examples.process: Error: Saying Reciprocal function is Even.

classifications.value: Drawing graphs crossing asymptotes or ignoring restrictions.
characteristics: Drawing Logarithmic function for negative x

image.caption: Figure 2.4.29: Error - crossing asymptotes.

examples.explanation: Correct: Asymptote means approach infinitely close but never touch.
Correct: Log domain is $x>0$. Graph does not exist for negative x.

classifications.value: Describing end behavior incorrectly for odd/even degrees.
characteristics: Saying Quadratic goes to $-\infty$ on both sides
Saying Cubic goes to $\infty$ on both sides
classifications.behavior: Incorrect prediction of long-term function behavior.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.30: Error - incorrect end behavior description.

examples.explanation: Correct: Negative leading coefficient flips it. Goes to $-\infty$ on both sides.

classifications.value: Confusing horizontal span (domain) with vertical span (range).
classifications.behavior: Fundamental misunderstanding of input vs output.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.31: Error - swapping domain and range.

examples.explanation: Correct: That is the Range. Domain is all reals $(-\infty, \infty)$.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Graph Errors

image.caption: Figure 2.4.32: Finding and fixing common graphing mistakes.

worked_examples.problem:
A student made the following claims. Identify and correct each:

a) 'The graph of $y = |x|$ is a smooth U-shape.'
b) 'The function $y = 1/x$ is continuous everywhere.'
c) 'The end behavior of $y = -x^3$ is up on both sides.'

step_by_step.explanation: Student confused Absolute Value with Quadratic.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: $y=|x|$ is a V-shape with a sharp corner at (0,0). $y=x^2$ is the smooth U-shape.

step_by_step.explanation: Student ignored the asymptote at x=0.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: $y=1/x$ is discontinuous at x=0 (vertical asymptote).

step_by_step.explanation: Student ignored the negative sign and odd degree.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: Odd degree means opposite ends. Negative means right side goes down. So: Left Up, Right Down.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) V-shape; b) Discontinuous at 0; c) Left Up, Right Down
worked_examples.concept_applied: Verify shape, continuity, and end behavior against parent function properties.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.33: Comprehensive guide to avoiding common graphing errors.

sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to the library of functions—recognizing shapes, identifying key features, analyzing behavior, and avoiding common errors—to demonstrate mastery of this foundational topic. These problems integrate visual recognition with algebraic analysis, preparing students for transformations and calculus.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 2.4.34: Practice problems covering the function library.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) Identify parent function: $f(x) = x^2$.
2) Identify parent function: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
3) Identify parent function: $f(x) = |x|$.
4) Domain of $f(x) = x^3$.
5) Range of $f(x) = x^2$.
6) Symmetry of $f(x) = x^2$.
7) Symmetry of $f(x) = x^3$.
8) Asymptote of $f(x) = 1/x$.
9) Intercept of $f(x) = 2^x$.
10) Intercept of $f(x) = \log(x)$.
11) Increasing interval of $f(x) = x^2$.
12) Decreasing interval of $f(x) = |x|$.
13) End behavior of $f(x) = x^3$ as $x \to \infty$.
14) End behavior of $f(x) = -x^2$ as $x \to \infty$.
15) Continuous or Discontinuous: $f(x) = 1/x$.
16) Continuous or Discontinuous: $f(x) = x^2$.
17) Domain of $f(x) = \log(x)$.
18) Range of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$.
19) Shape of $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$.
20) True/False: $y=|x|$ is smooth.

step_by_step.explanation: Quadratic parent.
Square Root parent.
Absolute Value parent.
Polynomial, all reals.
Parabola opens up from 0.
Even function.
Odd function.
Vertical and Horizontal.
y-intercept when x=0.
x-intercept when y=0.
Right side of vertex.
Left side of vertex.
Odd degree positive.
Even degree negative.
Break at 0.
No breaks.
Argument must be positive.
Principal root is non-negative.
S-shaped curve.
Has a corner.

worked_examples.final_answer: 1) Quadratic; 2) Sq Root; 3) Abs Val; 4) R; 5) [0,inf); 6) Even; 7) Odd; 8) x=0,y=0; 9) (0,1); 10) (1,0); 11) (0,inf); 12) (-inf,0); 13) inf; 14) -inf; 15) Discont; 16) Cont; 17) (0,inf); 18) [0,inf); 19) S-curve; 20) False
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of parent function shapes, features, and properties.

image.caption: Figure 2.4.35: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: The library of common functions includes Linear ($f(x)=x$), Quadratic ($f(x)=x^2$), Cubic ($f(x)=x^3$), Square Root ($f(x)=\sqrt{x}$), Cube Root ($f(x)=\sqrt[3]{x}$), Absolute Value ($f(x)=|x|$), Reciprocal ($f(x)=1/x$), Exponential ($f(x)=b^x$), and Logarithmic ($f(x)=\log_b x$) functions. Each has a distinctive shape: lines, parabolas, S-curves, V-shapes, hyperbolas, and growth/decay curves. Key features to identify include domain and range, intercepts, symmetry (Even=y-axis, Odd=origin), intervals of increase and decrease, end behavior (as $x \to \pm\infty$), asymptotes (lines approached but not touched), and continuity (no breaks). Polynomials (Linear, Quadratic, Cubic) are continuous everywhere; Rational (Reciprocal) functions have vertical asymptotes at zeros of the denominator; Radicals have domain restrictions (even roots $\geq 0$); Exponentials have horizontal asymptotes and range $(0, \infty)$; Logarithms have vertical asymptotes and domain $(0, \infty)$. Common errors include confusing shapes (Quadratic vs Absolute Value), misusing interval notation for increase/decrease (use parentheses), misidentifying symmetry, ignoring asymptotes, and swapping domain and range. Mastery of these parent graphs is essential for understanding transformations, modeling real-world phenomena, and preparing for calculus where graph behavior determines limits, continuity, and derivatives.
